name: WABOT-1
short_description: The world's first full-scale humanoid robot: bipedal walking, grasping hands, Japanese speech, and artificial vision — built at Waseda University under Professor Ichiro Kato.
long_description: Completed in 1973 at Waseda University's Department of Mechanical Engineering under Professor Ichiro Kato, WABOT-1 (WAseda roBOT-1) was the world's first full-scale humanoid intelligent robot. It could walk on two legs, grip and transport objects with its hands, communicate in simple Japanese through an artificial mouth, and measure distances and directions using artificial ears and eyes. Its capabilities were roughly comparable to those of an 18-month-old child. WABOT-1 defined the research agenda for the entire field of humanoid robotics, establishing bipedal locomotion, manipulation, speech, and machine perception as the core challenges that subsequent decades of robotics would attempt to scale.
ai_note: Rule-based control and early machine perception (distance/direction sensing via artificial ears and eyes); no learned AI, but early sensor-based intelligent behavior for its era.

images.caption: WABOT-1 on display, completed 1973 at Waseda University.
images.alt_text: WABOT-1, a large metal humanoid robot with two legs, two arms, and a structured head unit, displayed at Waseda University.

sources.title: Famous Robots at Waseda: WABOT-1 (1973) — The World's First Humanoid Robot
sources.access_notes: Official Waseda University article covering WABOT-1's completion date, Professor Ichiro Kato's role, capabilities, and historical significance. States completion year as 1973.

sources.title: Humanoid robot — History (WABOT-1)
sources.access_notes: Wikipedia's Humanoid robot article cites WABOT-1 completion as 1972 and describes Waseda University project inception as 1967. Year discrepancy with Waseda University's own source (1973) is noted as an open question.

interactions.title: Ask it a question and hear it answer in Japanese
interactions.description: A person could speak to WABOT-1 in simple Japanese and the robot would reply through its artificial mouth — one of the first machines built to hold a spoken exchange with a person face to face.

interactions.title: Watch it take steps across the floor on two legs
interactions.description: WABOT-1 walked on its WL-5 lower limbs in a slow, quasi-static gait that kept its center of mass over the supporting foot — a deliberate demonstration that a full-scale machine could balance and locomote bipedally like a person.

interactions.title: Have it grip an object and carry it
interactions.description: Using its WAM-4 hands fitted with tactile sensors, WABOT-1 could grasp an object and transport it — coupling touch sensing to manipulation rather than running a blind, pre-scripted motion.

interactions.title: Let it locate an object before reaching for it
interactions.description: Through its vision system — the artificial eyes and ears acting as external receptors — WABOT-1 could measure the distance and direction to objects around it, giving the limb-control system somewhere to aim.

interactions.title: Three separate systems acting as one body
interactions.description: WABOT-1 integrated a limb-control system, a vision system, and a conversation system at full human scale — researchers judged the combined faculties roughly equal to those of a one-and-a-half-year-old child, framing the whole agenda of humanoid robotics that followed.
interactions.source_label: Waseda University — Famous Robots at Waseda

interactions.title: Fetch-and-place by spoken request
interactions.description: Chaining the abilities WABOT-1 already had: ask it in Japanese to bring an object, let the vision system fix the object's distance and direction, then have the hands grasp and carry it back — a single errand stitched from its conversation, vision, and limb systems.

interactions.title: A guided lobby tour for visitors
interactions.description: Standing where it does today in Building 63, WABOT-1 could greet arrivals in spoken Japanese and step slowly toward a display while describing it — turning its walking, speech, and sensing into a simple docent routine.
